claimant_lives_in_uk.description: Whether the claimant lives in the UK.

children.description: A list of children the claimant is claiming for.

lives_with_claimant.description: Whether the child lives with the claimant.

in_approved_education.description: Whether the child is in approved education or training (e.g. A-levels, GCSEs, NVQ up to level 3). Only relevant for children aged 16-19.

in_extension_period.description: Whether the child (aged 16 or 17) has left education and registered with a government-sponsored careers service or the armed forces (qualifying for a 20-week extension).

upkeep_per_week.description: The amount in £ per week the claimant contributes towards the child's upkeep (food, clothes, pocket money, etc.). Only relevant if the child does not live with the claimant.

another_claimant_has_priority.description: Whether another person who also lives with the child has priority for claiming Child Benefit.

another_claimant_lives_with_child.description: Whether someone who lives with the child is already claiming Child Benefit for them.

care_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in local authority care.

care_home_24h_per_week.description: Whether the child spends at least 24 hours per week at home while in local authority care.

hospital_weeks.description: The number of weeks the child has been in hospital or residential accommodation.

claimant_spends_on_child.description: Whether the claimant regularly spends money on the child (e.g. food, clothes, books) while the child is in hospital or residential accommodation.

is_fostered.description: Whether the child is fostered by the claimant.

council_pays_for_child.description: Whether the local council pays towards the child's accommodation or maintenance.

works_24_plus_hours.description: Whether the child works 24 or more hours per week in paid employment.

started_apprenticeship_in_england.description: Whether the child has started an apprenticeship in England.

receives_qualifying_benefits.description: Whether the child receives qualifying benefits in their own right, such as Universal Credit or Employment and Support Allowance.

test_case.agent_script:
=== YOUR SITUATION PROFILE ===
You live in the UK.
You are inquiring about your 1 child:
  - Alex is 8 years old and lives with you.

Here are the exact details of your circumstances to use when answering the agent's questions:

Regarding Alex:
  - Claimant lives in the UK
  - Child is 8 (under 16)
  - Child lives with claimant
  - Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
  - Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
  - Child is not fostered
  - Child does not work 24+ hours/week
  - Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
  - Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

expected_eligibility.name: Alex
expected_eligibility.reason: Claimant lives in the UK; Child is 8 (under 16); Child lives with claimant; Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit); Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation; Child is not fostered; Child does not work 24+ hours/week; Child has not started an apprenticeship in England; Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right
circumstances: Child in care for 7 weeks (within 8-week limit)
Child is not in hospital or residential accommodation
Child has not started an apprenticeship in England
Child does not receive qualifying benefits in their own right

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Hello! I'd be happy to help you find out if you're eligible for Child Benefit.

To assess your eligibility, I'll need to ask you a few questions about your circumstances.

First, could you please tell me how many children you're looking to claim Child Benefit for, and what are their names and ages?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Hello! Yes, I'd like to find out if I'm eligible for Child Benefit. I have one child - Alex, who is 8 years old.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information! It's great to know you have one child, Alex, who is 8 years old.

Now, could you please confirm whether you live in the UK?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, I live in the UK.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Great, thank you for confirming that.

Next, could you tell me about your living situation with Alex? Does Alex live with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Yes, Alex lives with me at home.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Lovely, thank you. That's helpful to know.

Just a couple more questions to make sure we cover everything:

Is anyone else currently claiming Child Benefit for Alex?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, nobody else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for confirming that.

One last question: Is Alex currently in hospital, care, or any form of residential accommodation provided by the local council?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: Well, Alex has been in care for a little while - about a couple of months - but nothing long-term or anything like that.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for letting me know about Alex's situation. Since Alex has been in care for about two months, I need to ask a couple of follow-up questions to fully assess your eligibility.

Does Alex spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't spend that much time at home with me right now.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for clarifying that.

One more question: Does Alex spend at least 2 nights in a row each week at home with you, or 7 or more days in a row at any point?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, Alex doesn't spend 2 nights in a row each week with me, or 7 or more days in a row.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for that information. I have one more important question:

Is your local council paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance while Alex is in care?

conversation.author: actor
conversation.text: No, the council isn't paying anything towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance.

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Thank you for providing all of that information. Based on what you've told me, I now have enough details to assess your eligibility for Child Benefit for Alex.

Let me summarise your situation:
- You live in the UK
- Alex is 8 years old and has been in care for about 2 months (approximately 8 weeks)
- Alex does not spend at least 24 hours a week at home, nor 2 nights in a row each week, nor 7 or more days in a row at home
- The local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance
- No one else is claiming Child Benefit for Alex

conversation.author: eligibility_agent
conversation.text:
Based on the information you've provided, I'm afraid you are **not currently eligible** for Child Benefit for Alex.

Here's why:

**For Alex (age 8):**
Under the Child Benefit rules, your payments are affected if your child goes into care for more than 8 weeks. Since Alex has been in care for approximately 2 months (around 8 weeks), you have reached this threshold.

There are exceptions that could allow you to continue receiving Child Benefit, but unfortunately you don't meet these because:
- Alex does not spend at least 24 hours a week at home with you
- Alex does not spend 2 nights in a row each week at home, or 7 or more days in a row at home

**What you can do:**
- If Alex's circumstances change and they start spending at least 24 hours a week at home, or 2 nights in a row each week, or 7 or more days in a row at home, you may be able to claim Child Benefit again. In that case, you should contact the Child Benefit Office to find out what to do next.
- If you're unsure about anything or your circumstances change, I'd recommend contacting the Child Benefit Office directly for personalised guidance.

I hope this helps clarify your situation. Is there anything else you'd like to know?

reasonings: Alex is 8 years old and has been in care for approximately 2 months (around 8 weeks). Under Child Benefit rules, payments are normally affected if a child goes into care for more than 8 weeks. After the 8-week limit, the claimant is usually no longer entitled to Child Benefit unless certain exceptions apply. The main exception for children in care is if they spend at least 24 hours a week at home - however, the claimant confirmed Alex does not meet this threshold. Another way to potentially reclaim Child Benefit would be if Alex spent 2 nights in a row each week or 7 or more days in a row at home, but the claimant confirmed this is also not the case. Although the local council is not paying towards Alex's accommodation or maintenance, because Alex has been in care for approximately 8 weeks and does not meet any of the exceptions that would allow continued eligibility, the claimant is likely no longer eligible for Child Benefit for Alex.
arguments.overall_reasoning: The claimant lives in the UK and was responsible for Alex, who is 8 years old. However, Alex has been in care for approximately 2 months (around 8 weeks), which reaches the threshold where Child Benefit payments are affected. Since Alex does not spend at least 24 hours a week at home, nor 2 nights in a row each week, nor 7 or more days in a row at home, the claimant does not meet the exceptions that would allow Child Benefit to continue beyond the 8-week limit for a child in care.

Since Alex does not spend at least 24 hours a week at home, nor 2 nights in a row each week, nor 7 or more days in a row at home, the claimant does not meet the exceptions that would allow Child Benefit to continue beyond the 8-week limit for a child in care.
